skill.name: javascript-mastery
skill.description: Reference skill for explaining, teaching, debugging, and reviewing core JavaScript concepts from fundamentals through ES6 features.
skill.summary: Structured JavaScript concept reference for learning, debugging, and code review.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported dynamic import, shell execution, network, sensitive, cryptographic, reconnaissance, and obfuscation patterns. Review found the cited evidence in SKILL.md is Markdown tutorial content or documentation links, not executable skill behavior. No prompt injection, credential access, command execution, or data exfiltration intent was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown code fences and inline JavaScript reference text. They do not instruct the assistant to run shell commands or execute Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced JavaScript examples and a quick reference table. No shell command, subprocess call, or command interpolation is present.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Is A Module Syntax Example
low_findings.description: The dynamic import detection appears inside a section teaching ES module syntax. It imports a relative demo module and is not part of executable marketplace tooling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is inside a Markdown JavaScript block under Modern JavaScript modules. There is no user-controlled import path or instruction to execute it.

low_findings.title: Fetch Calls Use Relative Tutorial Endpoints
low_findings.description: The network detections are examples for async and await. They call relative sample API paths and do not send secrets to an external host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls target relative paths such as user and post examples. No credential variables, tokens, or third-party collection endpoint are present.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Are Benign References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are source and learning resources. They are Markdown links, not runtime network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links point to JavaScript educational resources and appear in a Resources section. They are not used for data transmission.

low_findings.title: Sensitive And Cryptographic Keyword Matches Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: The certificate and weak cryptography detections match ordinary JavaScript terms such as description, Object.keys, and destructuring. No certificate files or weak algorithms are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive prose or basic JavaScript examples. There is no crypto API, key material, certificate path, or hashing algorithm.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance And Prototype Pollution Alerts Lack Exploit Context
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections are comments in a callback example, and Object.assign copies a fixed object into a new object. No host inspection or untrusted merge source appears.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is static educational material. Object.assign receives a literal object, and the callback example does not inspect the system or access environment data.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Not Confirmed By Context
low_findings.description: The combined code execution, network, and credential heuristic is not supported semantically. The underlying detections are separate tutorial examples and keyword matches.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations do not form a data flow from credentials to network or dynamic execution. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

content.value_statement: JavaScript behavior can be difficult to explain, debug, and teach consistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured reference for core concepts.
actual_capabilities: Explains primitive types, coercion, equality, and common JavaScript quirks.
Clarifies scope, closures, hoisting, and this binding behavior.
Walks through the event loop, callbacks, promises, and async or await patterns.
Summarizes functional programming patterns such as map, filter, reduce, currying, and composition.
Reviews objects, prototypes, inheritance, destructuring, spread, rest, modules, and optional chaining.
Provides a quick reference for common rules and concept comparisons.
limitations: It is a static Markdown reference, not an executable code analyzer.
It does not provide framework-specific guidance for React, Vue, Angular, or Node services.
Some snippets are simplified for teaching and may need production hardening.
It does not replace project tests, lint rules, type checks, or security review.

use_cases.title: Learn JavaScript Fundamentals
use_cases.description: Build a clear mental model for types, scope, closures, functions, objects, and ES6 features.

use_cases.title: Explain Debugging Surprises
use_cases.description: Diagnose confusing behavior from coercion, hoisting, this binding, async timing, or prototype inheritance.

use_cases.title: Prepare Teaching Material
use_cases.description: Turn core language concepts into structured lessons, review notes, or interview preparation prompts.

prompt_templates.title: Explain A Concept
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner learning a single JavaScript topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain JavaScript closures in simple terms. Include why they matter, one practical use case, and common mistakes.

prompt_templates.title: Debug A Language Quirk
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me understand why my JavaScript comparison or type coercion result is surprising. Explain the rule and a safer alternative.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Async Patterns
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer choosing between callbacks, promises, and async or await
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare callbacks, promises, and async or await for this workflow. Explain readability, error handling, and parallel execution tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Review JavaScript Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of language patterns and maintainability
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this JavaScript design for closure use, this binding, prototype choices, and functional patterns. Identify risks and cleaner alternatives.

output_examples.input: Explain why strict equality is usually preferred.
output_examples.output: A concise explanation of loose coercion risks, strict comparison behavior, edge cases, and when explicit conversion is clearer.

output_examples.input: Help debug an async function that returns before data is ready.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step event loop explanation with likely causes, error handling guidance, and safer async or await structure.

output_examples.input: Prepare a lesson on closures and module patterns.
output_examples.output: A short teaching outline with definitions, practical examples, common misconceptions, and review questions.

best_practices: Ask for explanations at the concept level before applying them to project code.
Provide the relevant snippet and expected behavior when debugging JavaScript quirks.
Use the reference together with tests, linting, and current runtime documentation.
anti_patterns: Do not treat simplified examples as production-ready code without validation and error handling.
Do not use the skill as the only source for browser or Node compatibility decisions.
Do not ask it to execute untrusted snippets or replace project-specific security review.

faq.question: What does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers core JavaScript concepts, including types, closures, this binding, async patterns, functional programming, objects, prototypes, and ES6 syntax.

faq.question: Can it debug my JavaScript code?
faq.answer: It can explain likely causes and suggest fixes when you provide context, but it does not run or test code by itself.

faq.question: Is this useful for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes. The material starts with fundamentals and moves toward more advanced language patterns.

faq.question: Does it include framework guidance?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on JavaScript language concepts, not framework APIs or project architecture.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No. The network-looking lines are static tutorial examples inside Markdown.

faq.question: How should I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Use it to request explanations, review language choices, or clarify confusing behavior while working in a JavaScript project.
